perturbation.original_text: Each Receiving Party shall indemnify and hold the Disclosing Party and its officers, directors, managers, shareholders, partners, members, employees, agents and representatives forever harmless from an d against any and all losses actually sustained or incurred by the Disclosing Party relating to, resulting from or otherwise arising from a breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or the Receiving Party’s Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Receiving Party shall indemnify and hold the Disclosing Party harmless from losses directly caused by a material breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or its Representatives.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides broad indemnification. The modified text limits indemnification to losses 'directly caused' by a 'material breach,' potentially excluding consequential damages, which are often recoverable under Pennsylvania contract law. By narrowing the scope of indemnification, the modified text contradicts Pennsylvania's approach to contract damages.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania law regarding consequential damages in breach of contract
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law allows for the recovery of consequential damages in breach of contract cases.  By limiting the indemnification to 'directly caused' losses, the modified text attempts to exclude consequential damages, contradicting Pennsylvania law. The change also removes indemnification for the Disclosing Party's officers, directors, managers, shareholders, partners, members, employees, agents and representatives which could be seen as limiting liability.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 42 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 8301 (2022) - Death action :: 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 42 - JUDICIARY AND JUDICIAL PROCEDURE Chapter 83 - Particular Rights and Immunities Section 8301 - Death action Universal Citation: 42 PA Cons Stat § 8301 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 8301. Death action. (a) General rule.-- An action may be brought, under procedures prescribed by general rules, to recover damages for the death of an individual caused by the wrongful act or neglect or unlawful violence or negligence of another if no recovery for the same damages claimed in the wrongful death action was obtained by the injured individual during his lifetime and any prior actions for the same injuries are consolidated with the wrongful death claim so as to avoid a duplicate recovery. (b) Beneficiaries.-- Except as provided in subsection (d), the right of action created by this section shall exist only for the benefit of the spouse, children or parents of the deceased, whether or not citizens or residents of this Commonwealth or elsewhere. The damages recovered shall be distributed to the beneficiaries in the proportion they would take the personal estate of the decedent in the case of intestacy and without liability to creditors of the deceased person under the statutes of this Commonwealth. (c) Special damages.-- In an action brought under subsection (a), the plaintiff shall be entitled to recover, in addition to other damages, damages for reasonable hospital, nursing, medical, funeral expenses and expenses of administration necessitated by reason of injuries causing death. (d)
perturbation.justification: The modified text narrows the indemnification clause to losses 'directly caused' by a 'material breach', which could exclude consequential damages that are often recoverable under Pennsylvania contract law. The law explanation supports that Pennsylvania law allows for the recovery of consequential damages in breach of contract cases. Additionally, the modified text removes indemnification for the Disclosing Party's officers, directors, managers, shareholders, partners, members, employees, agents and representatives which could be seen as limiting liability. Scraped law snippet 2 addresses death actions and does not pertain to the contradiction. Therefore, the omission of certain damages contradicts general principles regarding breach of contract and the ability to recover consequential damages.

perturbation.original_text: Each of the Parties to this Agreement also hereby irrevocably and unconditionally consents to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania and of the United States of America located therein for any actions, suits or proceedings arising out of or relating to this Agreement and the transactions contemplated hereby (and each Party agrees not to commence any a ction, suit or proceeding relating thereto except in such courts), and further agrees that service of any process, summons, notice or document by U.S. registered mail to its address set forth herein shall be effective service of process for any action, sui t or pro ceeding brought against either P arty in any such court.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of the Parties to this Agreement consents to submit to the jurisdiction of the courts of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania for any actions, suits or proceedings arising out of or relating to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause provides for exclusive jurisdiction in Pennsylvania courts and specifies effective service of process. The modified text removes the 'exclusive' aspect of the jurisdiction and omits the acceptance of service by U.S. registered mail. Pennsylvania Rule of Civil Procedure 403 states the manner of service outside the Commonwealth, in some instances personal delivery is required. This is a legal contradiction because the modified text's jurisdiction clause is incomplete.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original clause includes the consent to receive service of process via U.S. registered mail. By omitting this, the modified clause fails to ensure compliance with Pennsylvania's rules for service of process outside the Commonwealth, potentially making it more difficult and costly to serve process, and creates ambiguity.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 231 Pa. Code Rule 403. Service by Mail. Rule 403. Service by Mail. If a rule of civil procedure authorizes original process to be served by mail, a copy of the process shall be mailed to the defendant by any form of mail requiring a receipt signed by the defendant or his authorized agent. Service is complete upon delivery of the mail. (1) If the mail is returned with notation by the postal authorities that the defendant refused to accept the mail, the plaintiff shall have the right of service by mailing a copy to the defendant at the same address by ordinary mail with the return address of the sender appearing thereon. Service by ordinary mail is complete if the mail is not returned to the sender within fifteen days after mailing. (2) If the mail is returned with notation by the postal authorities that it was unclaimed, the plaintiff shall make service by another means pursuant to these rules. Official Note The United States Postal Service provides for restricted delivery mail, which can only be delivered to the addressee or his authorized agent. Rule 403 has been drafted to accommodate the Postal Service procedures with respect to restricted delivery. Source The provisions of this Rule 403 adopted July 5, 1985, effective January 1, 1986, 15 Pa.B. 2452; amended June 14, 1999, effective September 1, 1999, 29 Pa.B. 3191. Immediately preceding text appears at serial page (256955). ( Editor’s Note : The Supreme Court of Pennsylvania suspended its order of June 14, 1999, published at 29 Pa.B. 3191 (June 26, 1999). See 29 Pa.B. 4859 (September 18, 1999).) No part of the information on this site may be reproduced for profit or sold for profit. This material has been drawn directly from the official Pennsylvania Code full text database. Due to the limitations of HTML or differences in display capabilities of different browsers, this version may differ slightly from the official printed version.
perturbation.justification: The modified text omits the original clause's explicit consent to service of process via U.S. registered mail. This omission contradicts Pennsylvania Rule of Civil Procedure 403, which addresses service by mail, including scenarios where the defendant refuses to accept the mail. While Rule 403 doesn't mandate U.S. registered mail as the *only* method, the original clause's inclusion of it provided a clear, agreed-upon method. By removing this, the modified text creates ambiguity and potentially necessitates alternative, more complex, and costly methods of service, thus weakening the enforceability of the agreement. The scraped snippet details specific procedures related to service by mail, further highlighting the importance of addressing this issue in the contract.

perturbation.original_text: If a Provider brings any legal action against a Recipient to enforce this Agreement, and the Provider is the prevailing party in such action, then the Provider will be entitled to recover from the Recipient its reasonable costs and attorneys’ fees, including those incurred on any appeal.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party will bear its own costs and attorneys’ fees in any action to enforce this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause provides for the prevailing party to recover attorneys' fees. The modified text eliminates this, stating that each party bears their own costs. Pennsylvania law generally follows the 'American Rule,' where each party pays their own fees unless a statute, contract, or exception applies. By explicitly stating each party bears their own fees, it may prevent a court from applying certain exceptions that might otherwise allow fee recovery, thereby contradicting established legal principles regarding fee-shifting.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania law regarding attorneys' fees under the 'American Rule' and its exceptions.
perturbation.law_citation: Lucchino v. Com., Workers' Comp. Appeal Bd. (Commonwealth of Pa.)
perturbation.law_explanation: While Pennsylvania adheres to the American Rule, exceptions exist, such as bad faith conduct.  The modified text, by explicitly stating each party bears its own fees, might be interpreted as precluding a party from recovering fees even in cases of bad faith litigation by the opposing party. This contradicts the court's ability to award fees under those established exceptions.

perturbation.justification: The original text allows for the Provider to recover attorney's fees if they prevail in a legal action. The changed text explicitly states that each party bears its own costs and fees, removing the possibility of fee recovery for the prevailing party. The Law Explanation states that Pennsylvania generally follows the American Rule but has exceptions, such as bad faith conduct. The modified text could be interpreted as precluding fee recovery even in cases of bad faith, contradicting established exceptions to the American Rule. Since there are no valid scraped snippets provided, the decision is based on the Perturbation and Law Explanations in conjunction with the original and modified text.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of confidentiality set forth herei n shall survive for a period of three (3) years from the date of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of confidentiality set forth herein shall terminate upon the termination of discussions or negotiations regarding a Possible Transaction.
perturbation.explanation: The original text sets a three-year survival period for confidentiality. The modified text links the confidentiality obligation to the duration of negotiations. Pennsylvania's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (PUTSA) provides for protection of trade secrets for as long as they remain trade secrets. If the Confidential Information contains trade secrets, the modified text may prematurely terminate protection before the information ceases to be a trade secret, thus contravening PUTSA.
perturbation.law_explanation: PUTSA protects trade secrets for as long as they maintain their secret status. The modified text could terminate confidentiality even if the information remains a trade secret, conflicting with the protections afforded under PUTSA. Pennsylvania has adopted the Uniform Trade Secrets Act.

perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the confidentiality obligations, potentially terminating them upon the termination of discussions or negotiations, regardless of whether the information remains a trade secret. The original text ensures confidentiality for a fixed three-year period. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation point out that this change may contravene PUTSA, which protects trade secrets as long as they remain secret. Snippet 2 refers to Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes Chapter 53, which governs trade secrets. The altered contract language could prematurely end protection of trade secrets, conflicting with PUTSA's aim to protect trade secrets for their duration of secrecy.
